reason: The item cleanly tests boundary-aware inference: it forces solvers to separate onset-temperature trends from runaway severity and to interpret a threshold-like degradation pattern without over-attributing causality. Decoys are largely plausible and target common overclaims (risk inversion; single-variable sufficiency; unsupported measurement limitation), though one decoy leans on an external-sounding metrology hedge that can feel stylistically “safe,” slightly reducing discriminative sharpness.

The item cleanly tests boundary-aware admissibility: what can and cannot be generalized from NMC/graphite identification results to LFP and to other operating profiles. Decoys are largely single-fault and plausibly tempting (chemistry transfer, sensitivity→physics, overfitting→no information), creating good expert discrimination. Minor weakness: some options use stronger modal verbs (e.g., “expected to hold,” “any parameter,” “all operating conditions”) that can make elimination slightly easier via wording rather than technical reasoning.

The item cleanly tests admissibility boundaries: what can and cannot be concluded from a low-rate OCV-style protocol, including selection bias from excluding low-capacity cells. Decoys are mostly single-fault and engineering-plausible (equilibrium overclaim, unsupported mechanistic extrapolation, and application-domain scope expansion). Some wording invites mild heuristic guessing (e.g., absolute terms like “guarantee” and broad claims about “all operating temperatures”), slightly reducing neutrality and discriminative strength.

The item cleanly targets boundary-aware reasoning about why parameter-sensitivity observations from a high-energy NMC/graphite case are not transferable to LFP, and it requires two distinct, evidence-grounded inadmissibility mechanisms (cell-type specificity and OCV-curve-driven sensitivity collapse). It strongly penalizes overgeneralization while keeping the response anchored to the stated framework behavior and sensitivity principles. Minor risk remains that as a short-answer item, responses may vary in phrasing or add extra, non-required reasoning, but the must-include constraints largely preserve tight grading boundaries.
